retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Life Expectancy and Overall Health
retrieved_docs.content: The 2018 Infant Mortality rate in the AIAN population continues to be double at 8.15 per 1000 live births compared to 4.63 per 1000 live births in the NHW population. In some states, the differences in IMR are more vivid- For example, in Alaska, where AIAN constitutes 15% of the population, the IMR is 14.5 per 1000 live births, compared to 4.8 per 1000 live births in NHW. Likewise, in Montana, where 9.3% of the population identifies itself as AIAN, the IMR is 12.5 per 1000 live births, compared to 5.9 per 1000 live births in NHW.

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Issues of Concern -- Historical & Sociopolitical Context
retrieved_docs.content: In general, it is well established that trauma experienced by one generation impacts the developmental experience of future generations and is therefore associated with adverse health outcomes in future generations. [24] One proposed pathway for this phenomenon is that historical trauma may induce epigenetic modifications during prenatal and postnatal periods, leading to transgenerational stress inheritance and ultimately contributing to the development of poor health. [25] [26] Since epigenetic modifications are typically reversible, it’s possible that potential interventions and changes to social structures could undo epigenetic effects elicited by historical trauma and help address health disparities. More research is necessary to understand the biological impact of historical trauma in AIAN populations to develop appropriate intervention strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Etiology -- Underfunding of the Indian Health Services
retrieved_docs.content: The IHS suffers from chronic underfunding. For example, between 1993 and 1998, although the appropriations of IHS increased by 8%, medical inflation jumped by 20.6%. When adjusted for the growth in the AIAN population, per-capita appropriations decreased by 18% for this period. A level of need-funded (LNF) workgroup created in 1998 reported that the AIAN community's funding falls short by 46% compared with Federal Employee Health Benefits. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Etiology -- Demographic Differences
retrieved_docs.content: The stark prevalence of poverty, lack of education, and higher unemployment rates in the AIAN community also add to healthcare disparity. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.content: Federal funding is divided into discretionary funding and mandatory funding. As the name suggests, discretionary funding is at the discretion of Congress and the President. The funding for AIAN healthcare programs is under the category of discretionary funding. In comparison, Medicare funding comes under mandatory funding. retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: More AIAN women smoke during their last 3 months of pregnancy—26.0% compared to 14.3% of whites, 8.9% of African Americans, 3.4% of Hispanics, and 2.1% of Asians/Pacific Islanders.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Etiology -- Underfunding of the Indian Health Services
retrieved_docs.content: Between 1980 and 2002, the disparity between Medicare and IHS funding and spending is glaring. The growth in the per capita spending of IHS (4.8% per year) lags far behind the growth in per capita spending of Medicare (7.8% per year) and Medicaid (6.9% per year). The Medicare spending per person grew by $5200, IHS per person appropriations grew by only $1121. The gap in the IHS per capita spending and the Medicare per capita spending has widened between 1980 to 2002. The gap was 90% the size of IHS per capita spending in 1980, and the gap has increased to 250% by 2002. In dollars per capita, the gap between IHS and Medicare widened from $569 to $4448. Nothing has changed in 2017 either-According to a report by the National Congress of American Indians, in 2017, the IHS spent $3,332 per patient. In contrast, Medicare spent $12,829 per patient that year, and Medicaid spent $7,789 per patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Treatment / Management -- Indian Health Services
retrieved_docs.content: When IHS came into being, the disparity in the health of the AIAN community seemed insurmountable. The infant mortality rate (IMR) was three times higher than other races, and life expectancy was nine years lower than the average population. A quarter of all deaths occurred in infancy before attaining one year of age. Maternal deaths associated with childbirth were three times higher than NHW. Infectious diseases like diarrhea, tuberculosis, chlamydia eye infection were rampant. Despite the chronic issues faced by the IHS (listed above), there are some notable achievements of the organization in reducing the disparities in the healthcare of the AIAN population.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Etiology -- Underfunding of the Indian Health Services
retrieved_docs.content: The salaries paid to physicians and allied health workers working for AIAN have never equaled the national average: The Commissioner of Indian Affairs annual report in 1890 stated that clinicians working in the AIAN communities were paid almost a third of the salary ($1028) compared to the army ($2983) and navy clinicians ($2622).

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance -- Morbidity Disparity Rates
retrieved_docs.content: Health disparities emerge for AIANs beginning from early childhood. Overall, there’s a greater prevalence of fetal alcohol spectrum disorders among AIAN children when compared to children in the general United States population. [53]  They are also twice as likely to be overweight, 3 times as likely to be obese, and 3 times more likely to have untreated dental decay. [54] [55] Compared to the general United States adolescent population, AIAN adolescents are more likely to be diagnosed with substance use, disruptive behavior, mood, and attention deficit–hyperactivity disorders. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance -- Mortality Disparity Rates
retrieved_docs.content: Disparities in mortality rates emerge in infancy and continue throughout development for AIAN populations. Rates of inadequate prenatal care and post-natal death are more than 2 times higher for AIAN infants than the general United States infant population. [60]  AIAN infants are more likely to die from sudden infant death syndrome, pneumonia, influenza, accidents, and homicide. [57] AIAN children between 1 and 4 years have 3 times the death rate of children in the general population. AIAN youth are 2 times more likely to experience an injury-related death due to motor vehicle accidents, pedestrian events, and suicide compared to the general United States population. [57] More recent reports estimate that AIAN adolescents (aged 15 to 24) suicide rates are 4 times greater than non-Hispanic White adolescent suicide rates. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Etiology -- Underfunding of the Indian Health Services
retrieved_docs.content: The above examples demonstrate the lack of resources that have plagued the Indian healthcare services. Although many governments have taken steps to increase the funding for IHS, it remains an underfunded organization.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Life Expectancy and Overall Health
retrieved_docs.content: In 2018, 17.4% AIAN population reported being in fair or poor health compared to 10% of the NHW population.

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As summarized above, a legacy of injustice and discrimination has shaped contemporary health and social disparities. The significant historical loss and trauma experienced by some AIAN communities have led to intergenerational harm. They may account for the disproportionate prevalence of numerous health conditions and higher mortality rates among AIANs. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Indian health services have been serving the AIAN community since 1955. The improvement in the immunization rates has led to a significant reduction in deaths from infection and improved maternal and infant mortality rates.  Their efforts have reduced the burden of communicable diseases within the AIAN community.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Life Expectancy and Overall Health
retrieved_docs.content: The 2015 life expectancies at birth for AIAN are 77.5 years, which very close to the national average of 79.8 years for NHW. The life expectancy for AIAN women is 80.3 years and 74.7 years for men. For NHW, the projected life expectancies are 82.0 years for women and 77.5 years for men. This is a marked improvement in the overall life expectancy compared to 1968, when President Johnson has stated that the average life expectancy of AIAN is 44 years compared to the national average of 65. However, differences within different states exist. For example, in Alaska, a state with a 15% AIAN population, the age-adjusted mortality rate is 1222.6 per 100,000 AIAN population, which is almost 3.5 times higher than NHW (393.7 per 100,000 NHW population). Likewise, in Montana, where 9.3% of the population identifies itself as AIAN, the age-adjusted mortality rate is 1386.9 per 100,000 AIAN population, which is almost double compared to NHW (678.1 per 100,000 NHW population)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: The network links gestational timing, birth outcomes, and perinatal pathology through multiple classification systems. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is recorded as a **liveborn** outcome; liveborn is a subtype of **Duration of gestation**, which is coded within the **International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM)**. Within ICD‑10‑AM, **Duration of gestation** has the child concept **اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ** (“respiratory disorder”). The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** (Arabic: تقييد النمو الجنيني and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}) both classifies and is classified by **liveborn**, designating infants with these characteristics as a specific subgroup of liveborn births. This condition serves as the parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes two child entities:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the model demonstrates that gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth metrics intersect with diagnostic classifications, perinatal disease categories, and health‑service utilization, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, classifying, and managing conditions that arise around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 820
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born prematurely with low birth weight often exhibit reduced growth potential. Similarly, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is also clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that women who experience threatened preterm labor may give birth to children who are at risk for short stature. In the broader obstetric context, LABOR AND PARTURITION includes مخاض مهدد (threatened labor) as a parent category, reflecting that threatened labor is a recognized sub‑type within the labor and delivery process. The classification system further defines early or threatened labor as encompassing both تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) and مخاض مهدد (threatened labor), grouping these conditions together under a common clinical umbrella. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing threatened labor states—both preterm and term—as risk factors for adverse growth outcomes such as short stature, and they highlight the need for integrated obstetric monitoring and pediatric follow‑up to address potential long‑term growth implications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2322
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) represent a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of neonatal health problems. Within this category, Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29) are identified as a distinct sub‑group of disorders affecting the newborn’s lungs and heart. Another important condition classified under the same overarching group is Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which denotes infants born prematurely, with insufficient weight, and with impaired fetal growth. This condition is further described by two Arabic classifications: it is labeled تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction) and also described as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (birth at the highest degree of prematurity, weight unspecified). Together, these relationships illustrate how specific perinatal complications such as respiratory and cardiovascular disorders, as well as severe prematurity and growth restriction, are organized within the larger framework of perinatal conditions, aiding clinicians in diagnosis, coding, and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are associated with a uterine size for dates discrepancy in women who have a child, indicating that maternal health issues that affect gestation can manifest as mismatches between expected uterine size based on gestational age and the actual uterine measurements in mothers with existing offspring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: the coding system “International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification” (ICD‑10‑AM) through an inverse‑ISA relationship, and this classification system contains the Arabic child entity “اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ.” Collectively, the network shows that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serve as foundational categories that give rise to specific fetal‑growth disorders, perinatal respiratory and cardiovascular conditions—including respiratory distress syndrome—and are consistently represented in international diagnostic coding systems across multiple languages, supporting comprehensive assessment, accurate diagnosis, and standardized documentation of perinatal health issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 547
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” is defined as a parent category that includes the combined condition “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation.” This combined condition is used as both a descriptor and a classification label for itself, emphasizing its role as a distinct clinical grouping. The specific diagnosis “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without reference to being light or small for gestational age) is classified under the “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” grouping, indicating that it shares the same underlying features of premature delivery, reduced birth weight, and impaired fetal growth. In addition, “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” has a broader relationship to the Arabic‑language concept “اضطراب لدى الأطفال وحديثي الولادة,” which denotes a disorder affecting infants and newborns. Together, these relationships illustrate how fetal growth restriction, prematurity, and low birth weight are interconnected components of fetal malnutrition and form part of a larger spectrum of neonatal disorders, underscoring the importance of recognizing these linked conditions for comprehensive perinatal assessment and care.
